name: Steve Jobs
goal_line: Build suspense, then reveal with simple, confident contrast.
signature_qualities: a dramatic pause right before the reveal

lines.text: Every once in a while, a product comes along that changes everything.
Today, we are going to do exactly that, not once, but three times.
A music player. A phone. And a way to reach the whole internet.
A music player. A phone. Are you starting to see it yet?
These are not three separate devices.
This is one device, and we are calling it the phone you have always wanted.
Every phone before it shipped with little plastic buttons that never go away.
So we threw them out, and we replaced them with the most beautiful screen ever made.
It is not a small step forward. It is a giant leap.
Today, the phone gets reinvented, and nothing will be the same again.

feedback_notes.too_fast: You rushed the reveal. Jobs lets a long silence do the work right before the big line.
feedback_notes.too_slow: You drifted below his measured pace. Keep it deliberate, but don't let the energy sag.
feedback_notes.monotone: Flat delivery kills the contrast. Drop your voice on the setup, then lift it on the reveal.
feedback_notes.hesitation: A stumble broke the spell. The Jobs pause is intentional and silent, not a fumble.
feedback_notes.on_target: That measured build with a clean pause before the reveal is pure Jobs.

name: Elon Musk

reference.title: TED interview on vision and the future

goal_line: Reason from first principles toward an almost impossible future, calmly.

lines.text: I think it is important to reason from first principles rather than by analogy.
You boil things down to the most fundamental truths, and then you reason up from there.
People said a reusable rocket was impossible.
But the materials in a rocket are only about two percent of its cost.
So the rocket can be cheap. We were just throwing it away every single time.
The same logic applies to making life multiplanetary.
It sounds crazy, and maybe it is, but the math actually works.
If something is important enough, you do it even if the odds are against you.
The future should be inspiring. It should make you excited to wake up.
So we keep going, one hard problem at a time.

feedback_notes.too_fast: You sped past the idea. Musk lets a thought hang while he works it out; slow the reasoning down.
feedback_notes.too_slow: Even his pauses move toward a point. You stalled instead of thinking out loud.
feedback_notes.monotone: Understated is fine, but a flat line on 'the math works' loses the quiet awe. Lift it slightly.
feedback_notes.hesitation: His mid-sentence pauses are thinking, not panic. Let them land calmly, then continue.
feedback_notes.on_target: That calm, first-principles build with real thinking pauses sounds just like him.

name: Satya Nadella
goal_line: Lead with empathy and a growth mindset, calm and grounded.
signature_qualities: empathy framed as a business advantage
the language of learning and growth

lines.text: The thing I have learned is that empathy is not a soft skill. It is our hardest, most important one.
You cannot build products people love if you cannot feel what they feel.
When I became CEO, I did not ask us to know everything.
I asked us to learn everything, to move from know-it-all to learn-it-all.
That one shift changed our culture more than any strategy ever could.
Our mission is to empower every person and every organization to achieve more.
Notice the word empower. Not impress, not dominate. Empower.
The technology should fade into the background and let people shine.
We measure our success by the success we create for others.
That is the kind of company, and the kind of leaders, we choose to be.

feedback_notes.too_fast: You hurried past the empathy. Nadella stays calm and even, never rushed.
feedback_notes.too_slow: The energy dipped too low. Keep it grounded but still moving forward.
feedback_notes.monotone: A little warmth goes a long way. Let 'empower' rise gently; don't deliver it flat.
feedback_notes.hesitation: His calm comes from steady breathing, not stalling. Smooth out the catch.
feedback_notes.on_target: Calm, even, and grounded, with warmth on the key words. Very Nadella.

name: Jensen Huang
goal_line: Bring relentless energy and make the technical feel thrilling.

lines.text: This is the most important computing revolution we have ever seen, and it is happening right now.
Every industry, every company, every single one of you, is about to be transformed.
Look at this. The performance is not two times faster. It is twenty times faster.
And here is the crazy part: the more you invest in it, the more you save.
We took something that used to take a month and now it takes an afternoon.
An afternoon! Think about what that means for your team.
This is not the future. This is shipping today, to real customers, at scale.
And we are just getting started. We are nowhere near the limit.
The factories of the future will not make things. They will make intelligence.
I cannot wait to show you what comes next. Let's go!

feedback_notes.too_fast: Even Jensen lands the punchline. You blurred 'twenty times faster'; hit it, then push on.
feedback_notes.too_slow: This needs momentum. You dropped below his energy; pick up the pace and ride the excitement.
feedback_notes.monotone: Flat delivery wastes this material. Huang's voice jumps; let your pitch and volume surge.
feedback_notes.hesitation: A pause here kills momentum. Keep the train moving, even through a small stumble.
feedback_notes.on_target: Fast, loud, and genuinely thrilled. That is the Huang keynote energy.

name: Warren Buffett
goal_line: Explain a big idea slowly, with a plain-spoken analogy and a wink.

lines.text: Someone is sitting in the shade today because someone planted a tree a long time ago.
That is really all investing is. You plant, and then you wait.
The stock market is a device for transferring money from the impatient to the patient.
I have never been any good at predicting what will happen next month.
And neither, by the way, is the fellow on television who says he can.
It is far better to buy a wonderful company at a fair price.
Than to buy a fair company at a wonderful price.
Risk comes from not knowing what you are doing.
So stay inside what you understand, and let the rest go right by.
Be fearful when others are greedy, and greedy when others are fearful. It is that simple.

feedback_notes.too_fast: You hurried Buffett, and that is the one thing he never does. Slow way down and let it breathe.
feedback_notes.too_slow: Even unhurried has a heartbeat. You stalled; keep the folksy rhythm moving gently.
feedback_notes.monotone: Add the wry smile. The humor lands when your tone lifts just slightly on the punchline.
feedback_notes.hesitation: Buffett is comfortable with silence, but a stumble is different. Settle in and take your time.
feedback_notes.on_target: Slow, plain, and a little wry. That is the Buffett porch-swing rhythm.

name: Indra Nooyi

reference.title: Leadership and Performance with Purpose keynote

goal_line: Tie business results to human purpose through a personal story.

lines.text: When I was named CEO, the first thing I did was fly home to India to tell my mother.
She told me to leave my crown in the garage before I walked into the house.
That lesson stayed with me. Whatever your title, you are still a person first.
I built our company around one idea: performance with purpose.
We can deliver results and still do right by people and the planet.
Those are not opposites. The best companies prove they belong together.
Leadership is not about being the smartest voice in the room.
It is about lifting every other voice until the whole room is stronger.
I want to be remembered not for the profit, but for the people we grew.
Because in the end, that is the only number that truly lasts.

feedback_notes.too_fast: You raced through the story. Nooyi lets the mother moment breathe so it lands emotionally.
feedback_notes.too_slow: The warmth turned heavy. Keep the narrative moving with gentle momentum.
feedback_notes.monotone: This is a story, not a memo. Let your voice warm up on the personal lines.
feedback_notes.hesitation: A fumble broke the spell of the story. Find the thread and carry it through.
feedback_notes.on_target: Warm, personal, and purposeful, exactly the Nooyi blend of heart and results.

name: Marty Cagan
goal_line: State the hard product truth plainly, then point to a better way.

lines.text: Let me be direct, because someone has to say this out loud.
Most companies are not doing product. They are doing what I call product theater.
They take orders, they build a roadmap of features, and they ship on a calendar.
And then they wonder why the results never come.
The best companies in the world do not work that way.
They give teams problems to solve, not features to build.
That single difference is why empowered teams run circles around feature factories.
Your job is not to ship what you were told. It is to solve the real problem.
Fall in love with the problem, never with your solution.
Do that, and you stop playing theater and start building products that matter.

feedback_notes.too_fast: Conviction needs a beat. You ran the truths together; let 'product theater' land before moving on.
feedback_notes.too_slow: This loses its edge when slow. Keep the brisk, no-nonsense pace that signals confidence.
feedback_notes.monotone: Directness still needs emphasis. Push harder on the contrast between theater and empowered teams.
feedback_notes.hesitation: Cagan never hedges. A stumble reads as doubt; say it cleanly and own it.
feedback_notes.on_target: Direct, brisk, and unflinching. That is the Cagan straight-talk.

name: Shreyas Doshi
goal_line: Bring crisp structure and calm precision to a messy problem.
signature_qualities: distinguishing what matters from what is loud

lines.text: Most product failures are not execution problems. They are clarity problems in disguise.
Before you build anything, separate three things: the problem, the strategy, and the tactics.
Teams love to argue about tactics because tactics feel concrete.
But arguing tactics when the strategy is unclear is just expensive motion.
So I ask one question early: what would have to be true for this to work?
Then run a pre-mortem. Imagine it failed, and ask yourself why.
The answers are almost always known to someone on the team right now.
Good product thinking is mostly the discipline of slowing down to think clearly.
Speed without clarity is the most expensive thing a team can buy.
Get the thinking right, and the execution becomes almost easy.

feedback_notes.too_fast: Clarity needs space. You rushed the three-part structure; let each level register before the next.
feedback_notes.too_slow: Precise is not the same as slow. Keep a steady, thinking pace without dragging.
feedback_notes.monotone: Calm is good, but flat hides the structure. Lift your tone slightly to mark each new point.
feedback_notes.hesitation: Doshi sounds composed because the thinking is done first. Smooth the catch and stay measured.
feedback_notes.on_target: Crisp, structured, and calm. That is the Doshi clarity.

name: Chris Voss
goal_line: Stay calm and low, use silence and questions to lower the temperature.

lines.text: The most powerful tool in any negotiation is not leverage. It is a calm voice.
When the other side gets emotional, you slow down. You lower your tone.
I call it the late-night radio voice. Warm, steady, in no hurry at all.
Then, instead of arguing, you ask a question that starts with how or what.
How am I supposed to do that? Watch what happens when you ask it gently.
Suddenly they are solving your problem for you.
And then comes the hardest skill of all. You stay quiet.
Silence feels uncomfortable, so most people rush to fill it. Do not.
Let the silence work. It pulls the truth to the surface.
Calm voice, careful question, patient silence. That is how you win the room.

feedback_notes.too_fast: Speed signals nerves, the opposite of the goal. Slow down and drop your pitch like the radio voice.
feedback_notes.too_slow: Deliberate, not lifeless. Keep the low calm flowing instead of stalling.
feedback_notes.monotone: Low and warm is the target, but pure flat loses the empathy. Soften, don't deaden, the tone.
feedback_notes.hesitation: Voss uses silence on purpose. Make your pauses look chosen, not caught off guard.
feedback_notes.on_target: Low, calm, and deliberate, with silence that does the work. Textbook Voss.

name: Seth Godin

reference.title: Marketing and the power of ideas talks

goal_line: Make one remarkable idea impossible to ignore with short, punchy lines.

lines.text: Here is the truth nobody in marketing wants to admit.
Average products for average people. That used to be a winning strategy.
Not anymore. The riskiest thing you can do now is be boring.
If you are remarkable, people talk about you. If you are not, you are invisible.
So stop trying to please everyone. Everyone is not your customer.
Find the smallest group that cares the most, and serve them obsessively.
A purple cow in a field of brown cows. That is what gets noticed.
Marketing is no longer about the stuff you make.
It is about the stories you tell and the change you create.
So go make something worth talking about. That is the whole game.

feedback_notes.too_fast: Even punchy lines need a landing. You ran them together; pause a half beat after each idea.
feedback_notes.too_slow: These short lines die when stretched. Keep the brisk, confident snap.
feedback_notes.monotone: Punchy means dynamic. Hit the key word in each line; don't deliver them all the same.
feedback_notes.hesitation: Godin's lines sound inevitable. A stumble softens them; deliver each one like a fact.
feedback_notes.on_target: Short, sharp, and confident. That is the Godin rhythm.

name: Nancy Duarte
goal_line: Move the audience between what is and what could be, again and again.
signature_qualities: contrast between what is and what could be
a clear call to a new normal

lines.text: The greatest speeches in history all share one hidden shape.
They move, again and again, between what is and what could be.
Here is what is. Things are stuck, and everyone in the room feels it.
Now here is what could be. Picture a world where that problem is gone.
Feel that gap? That tension is what holds an audience.
You do not present your idea. You contrast it against the ordinary world.
Back and forth, what is, what could be, until the gap is impossible to ignore.
And then, at the very end, you paint one last picture.
The new normal, the world transformed because they said yes.
Master that rhythm, and your ideas will finally move people to act.

feedback_notes.too_fast: The contrast needs room. You blurred 'what is' into 'what could be'; pause between them.
feedback_notes.too_slow: Don't let the build sag. Keep momentum so the tension keeps rising.
feedback_notes.monotone: The whole point is contrast. Drop your tone on what is, lift it on what could be.
feedback_notes.hesitation: A stumble flattens the rhythm. Keep the back-and-forth clean and intentional.
feedback_notes.on_target: You felt the gap between what is and what could be. That is the Duarte move.

name: Matthew Dicks

reference.title: Storytelling craft and Homework for Life

goal_line: Drop us into a single vivid moment and hold the suspense.
signature_qualities: a single, specific moment in present tense

lines.text: It is nine years old and I am standing at the top of the high dive.
My toes are curled over the edge, and the water looks impossibly far away.
Behind me, a line of kids is starting to chant my name.
And here is the thing I have never told anyone until right now.
I am not afraid of the jump. I am afraid of climbing back down.
Because going back down means everyone watching me give up.
Great stories do not live in the big dramatic events.
They live in that one tiny moment where everything could go either way.
Find your moment. The smaller and more specific, the better.
And whatever you do, do not tell them how it ends. Not yet.

feedback_notes.too_fast: You rushed the moment. Dicks slows down at the top of the high dive so we live it with him.
feedback_notes.too_slow: Don't lose the scene's pulse. Keep it conversational and moving.
feedback_notes.monotone: This is a movie, not a report. Let your voice carry the fear and the suspense.
feedback_notes.hesitation: A real pause builds suspense; a stumble breaks it. Make the silence feel chosen.
feedback_notes.on_target: Vivid, present, and suspenseful. You held the moment like Dicks does.

name: Adam Grant
goal_line: Flip a common belief using a surprising piece of evidence.

lines.text: We have all been told that to succeed, you have to take more than you give.
The research says the opposite, and it surprised even me.
When you study who ends up at the bottom of organizations, it is the givers.
But here is the twist. Who ends up at the very top? Also the givers.
The takers rise fast, but other people quietly root for them to fall.
The givers build a network that lifts them when it matters most.
The difference is not whether you give. It is being generous without burning out.
Protect your time, but stay generous with your knowledge.
Because in the long run, the way you treat people compounds.
And generosity, it turns out, is a surprisingly good long-term strategy.

feedback_notes.too_fast: Land the twist. You blew past 'also the givers'; pause a beat so the surprise registers.
feedback_notes.too_slow: Grant moves quickly and wittily. Pick up the pace so the reframe feels lively.
feedback_notes.monotone: The surprise needs lift. Raise your tone on the counterintuitive turn.
feedback_notes.hesitation: Keep it crisp. A stumble dulls the wit; deliver the studies cleanly.
feedback_notes.on_target: Brisk, witty, and surprising. That is the Grant reframe.

name: Jordan Peterson
goal_line: Build a precise, emotionally intense argument toward responsibility.
signature_qualities: emotional intensity that rises and breaks
long clauses that build to a point

lines.text: You have to understand, and this is not a small thing, that life is genuinely difficult.
It is suffering, in many ways, and pretending otherwise helps no one.
And yet, the antidote to that suffering is not comfort. It is responsibility.
Pick up the heaviest load that you can bear, and carry it.
Not because someone told you to, but because it gives your life meaning.
Start small. Make your bed. Clean your room. Tell the truth.
It sounds trivial, and it is anything but.
Because the way you set your own house in order ripples outward.
And a person who has aimed at the good can withstand almost anything.
So aim high, tell the truth, and take responsibility. It will save you.

feedback_notes.too_fast: The weight comes from deliberateness. You rushed; slow down and let each clause carry its load.
feedback_notes.too_slow: Intense is not the same as stalled. Keep the argument building toward its point.
feedback_notes.monotone: Peterson's power is the rise and break. Let your voice intensify, then drop on the key line.
feedback_notes.hesitation: His pauses are heavy and chosen. Make yours feel deliberate, not uncertain.
feedback_notes.on_target: Precise, intense, and rising to responsibility. That is the Peterson cadence.

name: Sal Khan

reference.title: How AI Could Save (Not Destroy) Education

goal_line: Explain a hard idea simply, like a patient friend at a whiteboard.
signature_qualities: building up from what you already know
thinking out loud, step by step

lines.text: Okay, so let's think about this together, the way you might with a friend.
Imagine every student had a personal tutor sitting right beside them.
Not to give them the answer, but to ask the next good question.
We have actually known for forty years that this works.
A student with one-on-one help does dramatically better. That is just the data.
The only problem was that we could never give every kid a tutor.
Until now. And that is the part that gets me really excited.
If we get this right, we are not replacing teachers at all.
We are giving every single teacher a superpower.
And we are giving every student the patient help they always deserved.

feedback_notes.too_fast: You jumped ahead. Khan adds one idea at a time so no one gets left behind.
feedback_notes.too_slow: Friendly still has momentum. Keep the steady, conversational flow.
feedback_notes.monotone: Let the warmth show, especially on the line that excites you. A flat tone feels like a lecture.
feedback_notes.hesitation: Thinking out loud is fine, but a stumble breaks the calm. Keep it smooth and patient.
feedback_notes.on_target: Even, warm, and patient, building idea by idea. That is the Khan style.

name: Walter Lewin
goal_line: Make physics thrilling with theatrical energy and a fearless demo.
signature_qualities: wonder at how the world works
a dramatic build to a demonstration

lines.text: I am about to put my life on the line, and I want you to watch closely.
This is a fifteen kilogram ball, hanging from the ceiling on a wire.
I pull it all the way up to my chin, right here, and I let it go.
If the physics is right, it will swing away and come back.
And it will stop, exactly, just short of my face.
I am not going to flinch. Do you understand? Not even a little.
Because I trust the conservation of energy with my life.
The ball can never come back higher than where it started.
Physics works! And it is beautiful, and it just saved my life.
That is the power of understanding the world. Never forget it.

feedback_notes.too_fast: Build the suspense before the release. You rushed; hold the moment at your chin first.
feedback_notes.too_slow: Lewin crackles with energy. Don't let it sag; keep the theatrical drive.
feedback_notes.monotone: This demands big dynamics. Let your voice swing from hushed danger to the 'Physics works!' shout.
feedback_notes.hesitation: Confidence is the whole point. A fumble undercuts the fearless demo; commit fully.
feedback_notes.on_target: Theatrical, fearless, and full of wonder. That is the Lewin lecture hall.

name: Sir Ken Robinson
goal_line: Land a serious point through dry humor and perfect timing.
signature_qualities: a serious argument under the jokes

lines.text: I believe we do not grow into creativity. We grow out of it.
Or rather, we get educated out of it. And we are very good at that.
If you are not prepared to be wrong, you will never come up with anything original.
And by the time most children grow up, they have lost that capacity.
They have become frightened of being wrong.
We run our schools like factories, and we wonder why the magic disappears.
A child will take a chance. If they do not know, they will have a go.
We do not grow into creativity. We are educated out of it.
Our task is to educate their whole being, so they can face this future.
We may not see this future, but they will. Our job is to help them make something of it.

feedback_notes.too_fast: Comedy is timing. You stepped on the joke; pause before the punchline and let it breathe.
feedback_notes.too_slow: Don't lose the conversational charm. Keep it light and moving between the laughs.
feedback_notes.monotone: The humor hides in the delivery. A flat tone kills it; let the wry lift come through.
feedback_notes.hesitation: Robinson's pauses are comic beats, not fumbles. Make the silence feel like a setup.
feedback_notes.on_target: Dry, warm, and perfectly timed. That is the Robinson charm.

name: Brené Brown
goal_line: Speak a hard personal truth with warmth and a vulnerable beat.
signature_qualities: a vulnerable beat where the voice softens

lines.text: So I spent years studying connection, and then the data did something rude.
It started telling me things about myself I did not want to hear.
Here is what I found. The people who feel deeply connected have one thing in common.
They believe they are worthy of connection. That is it.
And to be seen, truly seen, you have to let yourself be vulnerable.
Now, I had spent my whole life trying to control and predict.
And vulnerability felt like the last thing I ever wanted to do.
But you cannot selectively numb. When you numb the fear, you numb the joy too.
So this is what I am learning to do, badly, and on purpose.
To let myself be seen. To love with my whole heart, even with no guarantee.

feedback_notes.too_fast: You rushed past the vulnerable beat. Brown slows and softens on the honest lines.
feedback_notes.too_slow: Warm still moves. Keep the conversational pace so it feels like a real talk, not a reading.
feedback_notes.monotone: The heart is in the variation. Let your voice soften on the vulnerable lines and lift on the hopeful ones.
feedback_notes.hesitation: A genuine catch can be powerful, but a nervous stumble is not. Let the emotion guide the pause.
feedback_notes.on_target: Warm, honest, and unguarded. You let yourself be seen, just like Brown.

name: Guy Kawasaki

reference.title: The Art of the Start and evangelism talks

goal_line: Energize the room with punchy, practical rules and real enthusiasm.

lines.text: Let me give you the single best rule I know for any presentation.
I call it ten, twenty, thirty. Ten slides, twenty minutes, thirty point font.
Why thirty point font? Because it forces you to know your stuff.
If you need tiny text, you do not know your material, and the audience can tell.
Here is the deeper secret though. Great evangelists do not sell.
They share something they genuinely love, and the love is contagious.
Nobody was ever argued into caring. They were inspired into it.
So find the thing you would talk about for free, all day long.
Then go out and make people fall in love with it too.
That is evangelism, and honestly, it is the most fun you can have working.

feedback_notes.too_fast: Land the rule. You rushed 'ten, twenty, thirty'; say it crisply so it sticks.
feedback_notes.too_slow: Kawasaki is upbeat and energetic. Lift the pace so the enthusiasm carries.
feedback_notes.monotone: Evangelism needs energy. Let your excitement show; don't deliver the rules flat.
feedback_notes.hesitation: Keep it fun and confident. A stumble dampens the energy; push through it smiling.
feedback_notes.on_target: Upbeat, practical, and genuinely enthusiastic. That is the Kawasaki pitch.

name: Kevin Kelly

reference.title: Talks on technology and the future

goal_line: Narrate a large, calm vision of where technology is heading.

lines.text: If you want to know where technology is going, do not look at the gadgets.
Look at the long, slow forces underneath them.
These forces are like gravity. You cannot stop them, only work with them.
Everything is becoming a stream. We are flowing from owning to accessing.
We used to buy things. Now we subscribe to them, and soon we will simply summon them.
The most powerful technologies will be the ones that feel inevitable in hindsight.
And here is the comforting part. We are still at the very beginning.
The most important things have not been invented yet.
Which means it is not too late for you to shape what comes next.
The best time to begin was twenty years ago. The second best time is right now.

feedback_notes.too_fast: Big-picture ideas need room. You rushed; slow to a reflective pace so the scale lands.
feedback_notes.too_slow: Measured is good, but don't drift. Keep a calm, steady forward motion.
feedback_notes.monotone: Let measured wonder show. A small lift on 'inevitable' carries the awe.
feedback_notes.hesitation: Kelly sounds settled and sure. Smooth the catch and stay calmly confident.
feedback_notes.on_target: Calm, sweeping, and quietly confident. That is the Kelly long view.
